text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP OFFICE VISIT

ATTENDING: [GU Oncology Attending]
REFERRING PROVIDER: [Primary Care Physician]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Rising PSA on continuous ADT and Zytiga. Scheduled follow-up for ongoing management of locally advanced prostate cancer.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Gerald Hutchinson is a 70-year-old male with a history of high-risk locally advanced prostate cancer, diagnosed at age 67 with cT3bN1M0 Gleason 4+5=9 (Grade Group 5) adenocarcinoma, PSA 87.4 ng/mL at diagnosis, presenting today for a scheduled follow-up visit. He was initially treated with androgen deprivation therapy (leup 22.5 mg IM q3 months) combined with Zytiga acetate 1000 mg daily and prednisone 5 mg BID, followed by definitive IMRT to the prostate and pelvis (78 Gy/54 Gy in 39 fractions). He achieved an excellent initial biochemical response with PSA nadir of 0.4 ng/mL at 6 months and 0.2 ng/mL at 12 months on combined therapy.

He has now been on continuous ADT plus abi for approximately 36 months. Surveillance imaging at the 24-month mark (age 69) was reassuring with no radiographic evidence of distant metastatic disease and a PSA of 0.3 ng/mL. However, over the last 6 months, a clear upward PSA trend has emerged. Serial PSA values are as follows:
  - 6 months ago: 0.3 ng/mL
  - 4 months ago: 0.6 ng/mL
  - 2 months ago: 1.1 ng/mL
  - Today: 2.3 ng/mL

PSA doubling time calculated at approximately 3.5 months, indicating rapid biochemical progression. Testosterone has been confirmed castrate at 9 ng/dL on today's draw, excluding inadequate testosterone suppression as the explanation for PSA rise.

Mr. Hutchinson is otherwise asymptomatic. He specifically denies any new bone pain, back pain, hip pain, or rib pain. No hematuria. No lower urinary tract symptoms beyond his baseline post-radiation nocturia (1-2 times per night, stable). No focal neurological symptoms. He notes persistent fatigue, which has been ongoing since beginning hormonal therapy -- grade 1, manageable, not impacting basic activities of daily living. He denies dyspnea, chest pain, or new constitutional symptoms such as significant weight loss or night sweats. Appetite is intact. He remains ambulatory and lives independently with his wife.

Of note, given his strong family history -- father diagnosed with prostate cancer at 65, paternal uncle deceased from prostate cancer -- and his high-grade, high-risk disease, I have been planning to pursue hereditary cancer risk assessment. This is discussed further below.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: fatigue (grade 1), mild hot flashes (ongoing from ADT, stable), nocturia x1-2 per night (post-radiation, stable), mild erectile dysfunction (expected from ADT).
Negative: no bone pain, no new back or hip pain, no radicular symptoms, no headache, no visual changes, no dysphagia, no cough or dyspnea, no chest pain, no abdominal pain, no dysuria, no gross hematuria, no significant weight loss, no night sweats, no peripheral edema.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Prostate adenocarcinoma, Gleason 4+5=9, cT3bN1M0, diagnosed age 67
2. Hypertension -- well controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- on metformin 1000 mg BID, HbA1c last checked 7.1%
4. Former tobacco use -- 20 pack-years, quit at age 55
5. Osteopenia -- on calcium/vitamin D supplementation; dexa not recently repeated

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Retired civil engineer. Lives with his wife. Former smoker, 20 pack-year history, quit at 55. Rare alcohol use. No illicit drug use. He is physically active with daily walks. Has two adult children; both sons, family history disclosure counseling pending.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Father: prostate cancer, diagnosed age 65, survived (deceased from unrelated causes).
Paternal uncle: prostate cancer, deceased.
No known breast or ovarian cancer in family. No known colorectal cancer or other Lynch-related malignancies reported.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS:
1. leup acetate 22.5 mg IM q3 months (last injection [recent])
2. abi acetate 1000 mg orally once daily (with low-fat meal)
3. Prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily
4. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily
5. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily
6. Calcium carbonate 1200 mg daily
7. Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 128/76 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 16, Temp 98.2 F, SpO2 97% on room air. Weight: 183 lbs (stable from last visit). Height: 5'9".
General: Well-appearing, elderly male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversant and engaged.
HEENT: Normocephalic. Oropharynx clear. No scleral icterus. No cervical lymphadenopathy.
Chest/Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses. Normoactive bowel sounds.
Musculoskeletal: Full range of motion of bilateral hips and shoulders. No vertebral point tenderness on palpation of the thoracic or lumbar spine. No focal bone tenderness to palpation over ribs, sternum, or pelvis.
Neurological: Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact. Gait steady.
Lymphadenopathy: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.
Genitourinary/Digital Rectal Exam: Prostate without palpable nodularity or induration. Prostate volume appears reduced from initial examination. No recto-prostatic fixation. No perineal lymphedema.
Skin: No jaundice. No rash.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
PSA: 2.3 ng/mL (rising -- see trend above)
Total testosterone: 9 ng/dL (confirmed castrate; repeat draw confirms)
Complete blood count: Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, WBC 6.1 K/mcL, platelets 214 K/mcL
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 139, potassium 4.1, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR 75 mL/min), BUN 18, glucose 112, alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L, AST 22, ALT 19, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, albumin 4.0 g/dL
LDH: 178 U/L (within normal limits)
HbA1c: 7.1% (stable)

---

IMAGING:
Surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan obtained at the 24-month mark (age 69): no evidence of radiographic metastatic disease at that time. PSA was 0.3 ng/mL at that assessment. No new imaging obtained today -- will proceed with restaging workup given biochemical progression.

---

ASSESSMENT:

1. CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (CRPC) -- NEW DIAGNOSIS
Mr. Hutchinson meets PCWG3 criteria for castration-resistant prostate cancer. He has had serial PSA rises -- from 0.3 to 0.6 to 1.1 to 2.3 ng/mL over 6 months -- with confirmed castrate testosterone on today's labs (9 ng/dL). PSA doubling time is approximately 3.5 months, which is a worrisome kinetic. He has been on continuous ADT plus abi for 36 months and has had an excellent initial response, but has now exhausted the activity of this regimen. I have explained to him and his wife that while PSA is rising and we need to reclassify the disease as "castration-resistant," he feels well today, and importantly, he is currently asymptomatic without bone pain or neurological symptoms. We discussed that CRPC does not mean untreatable -- there are multiple effective options and we will be pursuing a complete evaluation.

2. abi -- DISCONTINUATION
Given confirmed CRPC with PSA progression on Zytiga plus ADT, I am discontinuing abi and prednisone today. Continuing Lupron to maintain castrate testosterone level; there is strong evidence supporting ongoing ADT even after progression to CRPC. Patient understands.

3. GENETIC TESTING -- GERMLINE BRCA/HRR PANEL ORDERED
Given his family history of prostate cancer (father, paternal uncle), his very high-risk disease at diagnosis (Gleason 4+5=9, seminal vesicle invasion, lymph node involvement), and the clinical importance of identifying germline HRR alterations (particularly BRCA2) for therapeutic decision-making in CRPC (ola, ruca eligibility), I am referring him today for hereditary cancer genetic counseling and ordering a germline hereditary cancer panel (Myriad myRisk or equivalent). Results are expected within 2-3 weeks. Cascade testing of his sons will be recommended pending results.

4. SOMATIC TUMOR MOLECULAR PROFILING
Given CRPC, I am also arranging repeat tissue biopsy (likely prostate or lymph node re-biopsy) with submission for somatic next-generation sequencing (Foundation One CDx or equivalent) to evaluate for actionable alterations including AR amplification/mutations, BRCA2 biallelic inactivation, CDK12 mutation, MSI/TMB, and neuroendocrine markers. This will guide sequencing of second-line therapy. Biopsy to be scheduled separately.

5. RESTAGING IMAGING
Ordering restaging bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Additionally, I will be ordering a PSMA PET/CT (68Ga-PSMA-11) to evaluate for occult metastatic disease given the known limitations of conventional imaging in low-volume CRPC. PSMA PET may detect nodal or osseous metastases not seen on bone scan or CT, and will also help inform eligibility for future PSMA-directed therapies if needed.

6. NEXT-LINE THERAPY
Deferring initiation of new systemic therapy pending results of germline testing, somatic NGS, and restaging imaging. Potential options under consideration include:
   - Xtandi (Xtandi) or darolutamide -- next-generation AR-signaling inhibitors
   - Lynparza (Lynparza) -- PARP inhibitor, FDA-approved in mCRPC with HRR mutations; highly relevant if BRCA2 germline confirmed
   - doce -- if visceral disease or rapid progression identified
   - Clinical trial enrollment

7. BONE HEALTH
Patient has known osteopenia at baseline and is now entering CRPC. Will reassess bone-directed therapy (denosumab vs. zoledronic acid) once metastatic status is defined by imaging. Ongoing calcium and vitamin D supplementation.

8. DIABETES / HYPERTENSION
Both stable per recent labs. Continued on current regimens. Note: prednisone being discontinued -- monitor blood glucose closely over the next 2-4 weeks as glucose may change.

---

PLAN SUMMARY:
- Discontinue abi acetate and prednisone today
- Continue leup acetate q3 months (next injection due [next scheduled date])
- Order germline hereditary cancer panel (Myriad myRisk)
- Schedule prostate/lymph node re-biopsy for somatic NGS (Foundation One CDx)
- Order: restaging bone scan, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, PSMA PET/CT
- Follow-up appointment in 3-4 weeks to review imaging, biopsy, and genetic testing results and finalize second-line therapy plan
- Continue amlodipine and metformin; monitor blood glucose after prednisone discontinuation
- Patient and wife counseled extensively today regarding CRPC diagnosis, next steps, and treatment options. Patient expressing appropriate understanding and engaged in decision-making. Questions answered.

Time spent with patient and family: approximately 55 minutes.

[Electronically signed]
[GU Oncology Attending Physician]
